coredata.dc:title: Optimization problem for in-situ heat generation estimation in lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Dauvergne, Jean-Luc
Nikulin, Artem
Jaime-Barquero, Edurne
Bekaert, Emilie
Palomo Del Barrio, Elena
coredata.dc:description: Knowledge of the thermal behavior of a lithium-ion battery is a key factor in ensuring its optimum performance. This paper presents a novel and non-intrusive method for accurately estimating the heat generated inside a battery during operation, based solely on surface temperature measurements. The originality of the approach lies in its ability to retrieve the total internal heat sources over time, without any prior assumptions regarding their profile, number, or spatial distribution across the cell thickness by solving an optimization problem with a regularized objective function, where the input is the measured surface temperature, and the output is the time-dependent internal heat generation. This method therefore combines simple and in-situ instrumentation, without requiring other characterization devices such as calorimeters, with a powerful non-iterative estimation method. The results obtained both numerically and experimentally show high accuracy over a wide range of conditions. In numerical tests, the relative error on the total estimated energy remained below 0.03% in most cases where effective thermal properties of the cell are known. To account for uncertainties in these effective properties, an enthalpic formulation was used, and the error in the estimated total enthalpy remained below 0.3% despite significant initial biases in the input thermal properties. Experimental validation using a dummy pouch cell with a controlled heating element confirmed these results, with relative total energy estimation errors not exceeding 8.2% for purely theoretical patterns and as low as 1.6% for a heat generation profile from a real calorimetric experiment, demonstrating the reliability and robustness of the proposed method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Heat generation
Inverse method
Numerical experiments
Experimental validation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 2D schematics of the simulated 1D configurations: a) layered structure of a cell encompassing the 9 elements described in Table 1; b) thin battery unit with 9 layers; c) thick battery with 123 layers. The red striped areas indicate the location of the internal heat sources.
Simulated patterns of internal heat generation. a) linear; b) cyclical; c) exponentially decaying; d) irregular with endothermic peaks; e) calorimetric profile.

Simulated temperature responses (both faces of the two batteries) with different internal heat generations profiles. a) to e): Battery unit with 9 layers, f) to j): Battery with 123 layers. The highest temperatures are those simulated on the surface closest to the source location. a, f) linear; b, g) cyclical; c, h) exponentially decaying; d, i) irregular with endothermic peaks and e, j) calorimetric profile.

Simulated inputs (dashed blue) versus estimated (solid orange) internal heat generations (power). a) linear; b) cyclical; c) exponentially decaying; d) irregular with endothermic peaks; e) calorimetric profile.

Measured surfaces temperature responses (in black) with different patterns of internal heat generation. Measured temperature of the environment (in blue). a) linear; b) cyclical; c) exponentially decaying; d) irregular without endothermic peaks; e) calorimetric profile.

Measured inputs (dashed blue) versus estimated (solid orange) internal heat generations (power) (a to e) and corresponding energies after time integration (f to j). a, f) linear; b, g) cyclical; c, h) exponentially decaying; d, i) irregular with endothermic peaks and e, j) calorimetric profile.
Thermophysical properties of the materials used to simulate a prototype pouch cell [25,26].

Results of the numerical experiments: root mean squares calculated between the real heat sources and the estimated ones for the different heat generation profiles and corresponding errors in energy and enthalpy.
Thermophysical properties used to calculate the effective properties of the dummy pouch cell [25,26].

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Roughly estimated considering the different components of the heating element.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of the experimental tests: root mean squares calculated between the measured heat generation profiles and the estimated ones for the different heat generation profiles and corresponding errors in energy.

head.ce:title: Optimization problem for in-situ heat generation estimation in lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Systems Engineering and Automation, School 10 of Engineering of Vitoria-Gasteiz, University of The Basque Country (UPV/EHU), Nieves Cano 12, 01006 Vitoria-Gasteiz, Spain

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Knowledge of the thermal behavior of a lithium-ion battery is a key factor in ensuring its optimum performance. This paper presents a novel and non-intrusive method for accurately estimating the heat generated inside a battery during operation, based solely on surface temperature measurements. The originality of the approach lies in its ability to retrieve the total internal heat sources over time, without any prior assumptions regarding their profile, number, or spatial distribution across the cell thickness by solving an optimization problem with a regularized objective function, where the input is the measured surface temperature, and the output is the time-dependent internal heat generation. This method therefore combines simple and in-situ instrumentation, without requiring other characterization devices such as calorimeters, with a powerful non-iterative estimation method. The results obtained both numerically and experimentally show high accuracy over a wide range of conditions. In numerical tests, the relative error on the total estimated energy remained below 0.03% in most cases where effective thermal properties of the cell are known. To account for uncertainties in these effective properties, an enthalpic formulation was used, and the error in the estimated total enthalpy remained below 0.3% despite significant initial biases in the input thermal properties. Experimental validation using a dummy pouch cell with a controlled heating element confirmed these results, with relative total energy estimation errors not exceeding 8.2% for purely theoretical patterns and as low as 1.6% for a heat generation profile from a real calorimetric experiment, demonstrating the reliability and robustness of the proposed method.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their high performance, such as high energy densities and power, cyclability and lifespan, lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries are an essential energy storage solution for many industrial applications, from electronic devices to electric vehicles. [1,2]. Maintaining or improving these key performances while preserving safety is a challenge that requires constant scientific and engineering developments. In particular, quantifying, understanding and controlling the heat generated by a battery during charging or discharging process, and therefore its temperature levels, is crucial to ensure optimal performance and lifespan of the battery trough, for example, the design of an appropriate Battery Thermal Management System [3–5].

Because of their critical impact on the Li-ion batteries performances, internal heat generation mechanisms have been extensively studied (see, for example, the synthesis works of Liu et al. [1], Choudhari et al. [2] or Raijmakers et al. [6]). They can be divided into two parts, reversible and irreversible heat, both induced by chemical and physical processes. As summarized by Liu et al. [1], and first formalized by Bernardi et al. [7], irreversible heat is mainly generated by the Joule heating caused by the current flowing through the battery’s internal resistance while the reversible part mainly originates from reversible entropy change throughout the electrochemical reactions during the charging/discharging processes (heat generation/absorption, respectively). Additional internal heat sources such as the heat of mixing or the heat of phase change exist but are generally neglected because of their minor contribution to the total amount of heat generated [8].

To precisely understand the heat generation behavior of a battery, it is essential to quantify the internal heat sources. This quantification is typically achieved by adjusting various parameters, including the battery's state of charge, the charge/discharge rate, and the ambient temperature. Hu et al. [9] classify the methods of internal heat sources estimation into direct and indirect approaches. The first category covers calorimetric measurements, including the use of accelerating rate calorimeters and isothermal heat conduction calorimeters in different modes (adiabatic, isothermal, isoperibolic). Bandhauer et al. [10] work offers a very clear overview of the different strategies used, as well as their limitations. The second category, which includes the present study, is based on comparing the thermal response of a battery in operation (using thermocouples [11–13], fluxmeters [14,15] or an infrared camera [16], for example) with a model of its behavior, to estimate the unknown internal heat generation. These methods are intended to be more versatile, enabling the in-situ study of a battery under realistic conditions. In addition, they are cost-effective, fast and easy to implement as they do not require additional equipment like a calorimeter. Nevertheless, their accuracy is highly dependent on the quality of the instrumentation and the ability of the model to reproduce the behavior of the system under study [9,11]. Additionally, these approaches generally require significant modifications to the battery's boundary conditions, for example, by attaching a fluxmeter to the battery surface [14] or by thermally insulating the cell [11], thereby deviating from real operating conditions. Finally, these methods are generally designed to estimate only the total heat flux generated by the whole cell, without the possibility of assessing it locally or excluding areas where internal heat sources may be significantly higher—without necessarily indicating a malfunction (e.g., close to the taps of a pouch cell). Therefore, the objective of this work is to propose a method that can be readily extended to both global and local monitoring, for instance by integrating additional sensors or using techniques such as infrared thermography. This approach also aims to combine the strengths of existing methods while providing minimally invasive or non-intrusive in operando monitoring and relying on the simultaneous measurement of temperature and the estimation of the corresponding heat flux, which is more likely to provide early indications of abnormal behavior than temperature measurements alone.

In this framework, this paper introduces an original method to estimate the total (i.e., reversible and irreversible) internal heat generation over time of an operating battery, solely based on measurements of its surface thermal behavior. The novelty of this work lies in the combination of simple in-situ instrumentation with a performant inverse modeling algorithm, without requiring any assumptions regarding the profile, number, or location across thickness of the cell of internal heat sources. By addressing this challenge, the proposed approach combines the advantages of non-calorimetric methods (versatility, low cost, easy implementation, etc.) with high accuracy, offering a crucial in-situ tool for testing batteries under a wide range of environmental conditions, for example, to improve battery thermal management and safety. After describing a thermal model that considers the thermal gradients within the cell, the internal heat sources and the associated assumptions, the proposed inverse method is presented. The latter involves solving an optimization problem [17] with a regularized objective function whose input is the temperature measured at the surface of the battery and output is a quantitative function of the evolution of total heat sources over time. To assess the accuracy of the heat generation estimations, two configurations were numerically tested: a minimal cell structure (including the coated electrodes, a separator with electrolyte and an aluminum-coated plastic casing film) and a more complex one with a hundred layers and thickness close to those of a cell phone battery, both for different profiles of internal heat generation. Finally, the proposed method has been experimentally validated by estimating, through simple surface temperature measurements, similar patterns of internal heat sources generated into a dummy cell containing all the layers of a prototype pouch cell and a regulated and monitored heating element.

To present and validate the heat generation estimation method proposed in this paper, a pouch cell configuration is considered. A pouch cell is a structure of parallel flat layers, which includes collectors, electrodes, electrolyte and separators.

The main assumptions employed to model the heat transfer within the cell are as follows:
•
The layers are isotropic, and their thermal properties (heat capacity and thermal conductivity) are constant; that is, independent of the temperature. This is appropriate if the cell does not experience extreme temperature variations.
•
The thermal contact between layers is perfect and thus the effect of the thermal resistances at the interfaces can be neglected.
•
The heat generated inside the cell can vary along the cell thickness (z-axis) but is uniformly distributed in the x-y planes, so that one-dimensional heat transfer conditions apply. This widely used assumption [6] may sometimes prove to be excessive for certain configurations (e.g. overheating in the tabs of a pouch cell [18]). The proposed estimation method would also be applicable in cases of non-uniform in-plane heat sources, provided that it is combined with the use of multiple thermocouples [19] or infrared thermography [20] to follow the thermal evolution of the cell at its surface. However, this scenario is beyond the scope of this work.

Eqs. (1)–(3) will be referred to as reference model hereafter and used in section 3 for numerical validation of the heat generation estimation method proposed in this paper. Finally, initial condition is set to

Applying volume averaging method [21] to Eqs. (1)–(3) leads to a heat transfer model in which the original multiphase system is replaced by an equivalent homogeneous medium with effective thermal properties:

The validity of this approach depends on several restrictions regarding length and time scales that are discussed elsewhere [21] and it has been already applied with success for homogenization of multilayer systems [22]. In this paper, model (4) is used for heat sources estimation purposes and its validity is proven in section 3 through the analysis of quality of the obtained estimates.

The spatial discretization (finite volume method in this paper) of Eq. (4) leads to the following state-space model:

described the connection trough the heat losses between the cell and the environment.

Retrieving
σ ( t )
from temperature measured data is approached by solving the optimization problem that consists in finding

constrained by the averaged heat transfer model (4) and assuming that the effective thermal properties of the cell, as well as heat transfer coefficient h , are known. The first term on the right side of Eq. (7) represents the quadratic error in the measured temperatures
T m
( t )
on the left and right faces of the cell (
m = l e f t , r i g h t
) and the simulated temperatures
y m
t
at those locations. The second term is a Tikhonov regularization term with serves to mitigate the effect of measurement noise on the quality of the estimations [17]. μ is the so-called Tikhonov parameter.

The demonstration of the different steps of the proposed inverse method for retrieving the heat sources and its application to other fields as the thermophysical characterization of phase-change materials have been detailed in earlier works by the same authors [19,23]. Here, we recall the main steps of this approach and its adaptation to the particularities of the studied batteries systems.

The numerical approximation of Eq. (7) can be written as:
(8)

Applying Lagrange’s theory and rules from calculus of variations, it can be proven [19] that the minimum of J is achieved when the following three problems are satisfied simultaneously:

Finally, the inverse problem (12) is a non-iterative linear and stationary state model whose input is the temperature measured on the sample and the output are the unknown internal heat sources
σ
t
divided by the volumetric specific heat of each finite volume.

Two comments can already be made.
•
To obtain the volumetric power generated by the cell, it is necessary to multiply the estimated sources by this specific heat (see Eq. (5)), so the error will be directly proportional to this property. To overcome such problem, it is worth noting that Eq. (1) can be rewritten in the following enthalpic form [7]:
(13)
∂ H

where
H
n × 1
is the vector of enthalpy
( J )
and V the volume of the battery
(
m
3
)
. This formulation has two advantages: on the one hand, accurate knowledge of the volumetric heat capacity is not necessary, and on the other, the temporal integration of the estimated source terms makes the results obtained more resistant to the measurement noise.
•
Boundary conditions (thermal exchanges between the battery and the environment it is placed in) are also a potential source of error. These must therefore be evaluated by measurement, estimation or by using correlations available in literature. In this work, an estimation based on a minimization procedure after cancellation of internal sources was adopted. A non-linear least squares solver was used to find the global heat losses coefficient that minimizes the heat sources at the end of an experiment. While this approach proved well suited to the configurations tested in this work, as shown in the following, it may require more specific work in other cases.

To assess the merits and limits of the proposed approach, a thin battery unit was first modelled by using the “complete model” (i.e., modelling of each layer and precise localization of the heat sources within the battery). This cell encompasses 9 layers: two electrodes coated on both sides with two different materials and a separator wetted by the electrolyte, all encased within a coffee bag cover (see Fig. 1 a and b). The total thickness of the cell is 0.926 mm.
The thermophysical properties of each layer are provided in Table 1 .
For this numerical example, internal heat sources were arbitrarily located in layers 4, 5, 6 and 7 for the simulation of the global thermal behavior of the pouch cell.

A second numerical example was developed to model a more complex system with non-negligible internal temperature gradients. This battery encompasses 123 layers: layers from 2 to 8 in Table 1 were repeated 15 times and encased between layers 1 and 9 (sealing). Internal heat sources were arbitrarily located in layers 12 to 16 (see Fig. 1 c). The total thickness of this multilayer is 5.041 mm.

For both examples, coefficients of heat exchanges with the surrounding were set at
15 W .
m
- 2
.
K
- 1
while environment and initial temperatures were fixed at 20 °C.

Three different patterns of internal heat sources, depicted in Fig. 2 , were tested as suggested by Bazinski and Wang [16]: a linear, a cyclic and an exponentially decaying one. In addition, a more irregular profile with some endothermic peaks and another from a calorimetric experiment [27] were tested to realistically complete the three theoretical cases. All these inputs were generated with a time step of 0.1 s.
The simulated thermal responses on both surfaces of the two batteries using the “complete model” are shown in Fig. 3 .

It is worth noting that additional numerical tests were carried out to assess the impact of adding measurement noise to the simulated data or considering a significant error in the evaluation of the effective thermal properties used in the “effective model” on the accuracy of the proposed method. These results are discussed in the following.
All simulations and data processing were conducted using GNU Octave 7.3.0 [28] with the “optim” and “control” packages.

To assess the quality of the total heat generation estimations a first criterion based on the root mean square of the difference between the real heat sources (inputs of the “complete model”) and the estimated ones (using the “effective model”) was used:
(16)
RMS =
1
n t
∑
i = 1
n t
X
real
t i
-
X
estimated
t i
2
where
n t
is the number of time steps and X is the time-vector of the heat generation. X can be the power ( σ , in W ), the corresponding energy (
E =
∫
τ = 0
t
σ
τ
d τ
, in J ) or the enthalpy (see Eq. (14), in J ). A second criterion for assessing relative errors in quantifying the difference between the real and estimated total energy or enthalpy generated during a complete experiment was also used:
(17)
Error
( % )
=
X
real
t
end
-
X
estimated
t
end
X
real
t
end
where X is the final value (at
t
end
) of the heat generation (energy E or enthalpy H ) during an experiment.
The results obtained for all the tested configurations are summarized in Table 2 .

In the first step of this numerical study, all the parameters of the effective model are known, except for the heat source term whose locations and behavior in time are unknown. After simulation of the thermal behavior of battery surfaces for the different tested patterns with the “complete model” (
T m
t
), the optimization problem was addressed by minimizing the difference with
y
t
calculated with the exact effective thermal properties corresponding to the set of layers making up the battery unit (
k
effective
= 0.5546 W .
m
- 1
.
K
- 1
and
ρ c
effective
= 2.2193 ×
10
6
J .
m
- 3
.
K
- 1
) and averaging the heat source term on the total thickness of the cell. Fig. 4 shows the excellent agreement between the inputs of the complete model and the estimated heat sources for all the configurations tested.

The patterns of heat generation with the sharpest changes (exponential and irregular profiles) lead to slightly less accurate estimates due to the chosen time step. A reduction in the latter can improve the results obtained. However, in the configurations tested, none of the estimated total energies (after time integration of the estimated powers) has an error greater than 0.02 % (see Table 2).

When adding a random noise (amplitude 0.1 °C, centered on 0) to the simulated measurements
T m
t
, the results on the estimated powers are also polluted by this noise (see Fig. S1 in supplementary material). Nevertheless, this noise does not have a significant impact on the calculated total energies (error of less than 0.03 %, except for the exponential profile with an error of less than 2.3 %) with an excellent reconstruction of the different energy patterns (see corresponding RMS in Table 2).

As underlined in section 2, a lack of knowledge of the effective thermal properties of the battery under study should have a direct impact on the quality of the estimation, particularly with regard to the volumetric heat capacity. As shown in Fig. S2 (see supplementary material), the estimate of the internal power generated by the battery is particularly biased by an error (100 % in the case tested) in the volumetric heat capacity used to predict temperature responses (
y
t
in Eq. (7)) when solving the optimization problem. As mentioned above, one strategy for mitigating this error is to express the estimation results using an enthalpic formulation. This approach makes it possible to correct the initial error in volumetric heat capacity, while greatly reducing the impact of measurement noise due to the temporal integration of the estimated source terms (see Eq. (14)). While errors in estimated total energy can reach 35 %, the corresponding errors in total enthalpy drop drastically to no more than 0.3 % (see Table 2). Therefore, the enthalpic formulation combines the battery's storage capacity with its internal sources, making it a powerful tool for completely describing battery behavior in a single function.

Several additional comments can be made. Firstly, it should be noted that the most significant errors, outside the enthalpy formulation, logically concern the internal heat profiles most sensitive to the volumetric heat capacity. Thus, the realistic calorimetric profile can be accurately estimated, both in power and energy terms. On the other hand, an error in the effective thermal conductivity of the battery under study has very little impact on the accuracy of the estimates. In the tested configurations,
k
effective
was set 10 times higher and lower than the real value. This remark confirms an observation made in previous work by the authors [18]. It is also recommended, when knowledge of effective thermal conductivity is uncertain, to overestimate the value that will lead to a better reconstruction of the source terms (see Table 2). This strategy was used in the experimental validation presented below: while the roughly estimated effective thermal conductivity is
2 W .
m
- 1
.
K
- 1
, it was arbitrarily set at
10 W .
m
- 1
.
K
- 1
.

The final step of this numerical study concerns the presence of non-negligible internal thermal gradients, as is the case with the 123-layer battery (see Fig. 3, f) to j)). As the proposed method takes into consideration these gradients, the reconstruction of the different heat generation profiles remains accurate. The errors on the estimated total energy do not exceed 1.6 %. In addition, this error is overestimated due to a step in the proposed method, namely the resolution of the Riccati equation. This is calculated at infinite-horizon and then the last terms of the vector containing the estimated source must be truncated. In the cases tested, the number of truncated terms should be higher to eliminate the divergence of the estimation at long times (se Fig. S3 in supplementary material). This too low truncation has not been deliberately corrected to illustrate this particularity of the proposed method, but the results obtained after truncation are summarized in Table 2, column “123-layers battery with truncation” and their clear improvement can be appreciated.

In a final step, the proposed method was validated experimentally using a dummy pouch cell including a controlled heating element. The main advantages of this approach are its ability to generate internal heat source patterns similar to those tested numerically for a system very close to a real pouch cell, as well as to verify the accuracy of the estimates without relying on additional devices (e.g., accelerating rate or isothermal calorimeters) which have their own measurement uncertainties and usually also involve heating elements for their calibration and experimental procedure [9,12,29].

The main elements of the experimental set-up implemented are (see Fig. 5 ):
•
the dummy pouch cell similar to the numerically simulated battery unit, into which a heating element has been integrated,
•
a programmable power supply to impose thermal dissipation by Joule effect patterns to the heating element,
•
a power supply output control device,
•
3 thermocouples: two dedicated to measuring the thermal behavior of the cell on each of its faces, and a third to monitor the temperature of the environment.

The dummy pouch cell (see Fig. S4 in supplementary material) is a multilayer comprising two electrodes (copper and aluminum), both coated with a layer of graphite coating, a heating element (17.0 Ω from Captec) containing a resistive nickel wire between two layers of Kapton, and one side covered with a thin copper foil to homogenize the heat flux generated by Joule effect. Two layers of standard coffee bag were then placed at each end of this multilayer. The different layers were fixed together by a thin layer of thermal paste. The total thickness of the cell is 0.75 mm for a surface of 4 x 4 cm2.

The effective thermal properties of this dummy pouch cell were estimated rather roughly, considering the probable variations in thickness of the various layers of thermal paste and the complexity induced by the heating element. As previously mentioned, these properties were calculated using mixing laws, thermal properties summarized in Table 3 and a serial thermal resistance model. The calculated thermal conductivity is
2 W .
m
- 1
.
K
- 1
and the volumetric specific heat is
2 ×
10
6
J .
m
- 3
.
K
- 1
.

The output programable power supply (EA Elektro-Automatik GMBH, EA-PSI 9080–40 DT) was monitored by an INA219 current sensor (Adafruit Industries). The patterns described in the previous subsection (Fig. 2) have been programmed to be reproduced by the power supply, except for the endothermic peaks as a heating element cannot reproduce them. In these cases, negative values have been set to zero. Data recorded by the current sensor was used to calculate the power generated into the cell (see Fig. S5 in supplementary material) and compared to the results obtained by solving the proposed optimization problem with the measured temperatures.

Two thermocouples (K type, 0.08 mm diameter) were fixed to the center of the two 4 x 4 cm2 surfaces of the dummy cell with a piece of Kapton tape. The heating element's power cable has been used to suspend the dummy cell vertically, to achieve comparable heat exchanges with the environment on each side. The third thermocouple, same type and diameter, was fixed close to the setup to monitor the temperature of the environment. Data acquisition was carried out using 3 thermocouple amplifiers MAX31856 with integrated cold-junction compensation (Adafruit Industries). The recorded thermal behaviors are depicted in Fig. 6 . It may be noted that the variations in ambient temperature recorded during the longest test (“calorimetric profile”, Fig. 6-e, blue curve) are mostly linked to the presence of the dummy cell relatively close to the thermocouple, but also to the laboratory's air conditioning. Variations in this temperature do not present any particular difficulty when solving the proposed optimization problem, as this parameter can vary over time.

In addition to the software of the programmable power supply (EA Power Control – V2.22), a dedicated one for temperature and current/voltage acquisition (at a frequency of 1 Hz) was coded using Python 3.7.3.

Fig. 7 shows the measured heat generation profiles and the estimated ones, in power and energy after time integration, while Table 4 summarize the corresponding root mean squares (see Eq. (16)) and error on the total energy (see Eq. (17)). As the estimation of the volumetric heat capacity can be biased, the enthalpic formulation was not used in this step.

As shown with the numerical study, the experimental results prove the ability of the proposed method to accurately reconstruct the different heat generation profiles in power. Corresponding energy profiles show a perfectible estimate of the heat exchange with the environment, which tends to slightly deform the latter after heat dissipation stops. A more efficient estimation method that focuses on this parameter may be the subject of future work. The errors on the total energy do not exceed 8.2 % for all the tested profiles. It should also be noted that, again, the realistic “calorimetric” profile leads to very good estimation accuracy (error of 1.6 %). In addition, an uncertainty analysis was conducted for all the tested patterns (details on the key parameters influencing the comparison between the estimated and the measured internal heat generation are supplied in supplementary material). The corresponding errors are summarized in Table 4.

This work introduces a novel and non-disruptive method for retrieving the total internal heat generation of a lithium-ion battery during operation, based solely on surface temperature measurements. This method combines simple in-situ instrumentation with a non-iterative algorithm, formulated as an optimization problem with a regularized objective function. A key originality of the approach is that it does not rely on any prior assumptions regarding the location, number, profile, or thermal nature (exothermic/endothermic) of the internal heat sources within the cell. In addition, this method is particularly robust to the impact of parameters such as measurement noise or a lack of knowledge of the effective thermal properties of the cell, a strong limitation of existing estimation techniques. The proposed approach then combines the simplicity and affordability of non-calorimetric methods with high accuracy, providing a valuable in-situ tool for testing batteries in various conditions, such as comparing different technologies, improving a battery thermal management system or tracking anomalous behaviors. This method was first assessed through a numerical study where the impact of different parameters such as the addition of measurement noise, a biased knowledge of the battery's effective thermal properties or the presence of thermal gradients associated with thick cells was investigated.

This numerical study demonstrated the accuracy of the proposed method in an ideal case, with errors in the total energy dissipated by the battery of less than 0.02 % for a thin unit and 0.28 % for a thick battery with internal gradient, and excellent prediction of the shape of the simulated different patterns. Subsequently, it was shown that simple temporal integration of the estimated powers (i.e., the corresponding energies) leads to similar accuracy. As the proposed method does not require any assumptions to be made concerning the internal heat sources, recourse to effective thermal properties and averaging of the estimated sources over the thickness of the battery is necessary. The numerical study showed a negligible impact of a large error (one order of magnitude) on the effective thermal conductivity used. It was also noted that, in case of uncertainty, an overestimation of this parameter is preferable to obtain accurate estimations. On the other hand, errors in specific volume capacity logically have a strong impact on the quality of estimation. When this parameter cannot be known with reasonable precision, it has been proposed to use an enthalpic formulation. The latter has the dual advantage of requiring time integration of the estimated sources, thereby increasing the resistance to the measurement noise, and of considering the equivalent storage capacity of the battery under test. Thus, this formulation proves to be a powerful tool to describe the thermal behavior of a battery in operation.

Finally, the results from the numerical study were confirmed by testing similar heat generation patterns in a dummy pouch cell encompassing all the layers of a prototype pouch cell, with similar thermal properties, and a regulated and monitored heating element. This validation showed good agreement between the estimated profiles and the monitored ones.

By combining defect generation and heat generation footprint, the proposed method is of potential interest for identifying and diagnosing battery degradation in charge/discharge cycling tests or under real operating conditions thanks to the simple instrumentation required and the accuracy of the estimated profiles. In addition, its adaptation to the processing of data from infrared thermography is particularly interesting. The pixel-by-pixel processing of apparent surface temperatures should make it possible the consideration of non-uniformly distributed sources in the plane of a cell, account for the diffusion effects, obtain maps of heat generation over time and thus consider the structural heterogeneities of a battery (e.g., the tabs) and the hot spots generated during a degradation process.

Jean-Luc Dauvergne: Conceptualization, Investigation, Software, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Artem Nikulin: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Edurne Jaime-Barquero: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Emilie Bekaert: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Elena Palomo Del Barrio: Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Fig. 1
